Digitize Elections.

Mandate Electronic Transmission of Results from Polling Booths.

Electronic transmission of election results is possible and should be made mandatory by law.

The election results to be transmitted electronically are from hard copies of duly signed election results from the polling booths. 

The hard copies should be scanned to the INEC office, and party agents should also get copies.

Constraints of no light and network in remote areas are no longer tenable due to the availability of solar-enabled power banks and alternative internet services.

comment_text:
On January 23, 2026, in Westminster, Colorado, police arrested 40-year-old Thomas Michael Raiola for driving under the influence (DUI) after he was caught driving recklessly. The situation escalated when, upon being handcuffed, Raiola informed officers that three children were hiding in the trunk of his car. 
Here are the details of the incident based on police bodycam footage and reports:
The Stop: A Westminster Police officer observed a vehicle driving recklessly near City Center Drive and 93rd Avenue. Upon stopping the car, the officer noticed a strong smell of alcohol, and Raiola appeared intoxicated.
Admission of Guilt: During the stop, Raiola admitted to the officer that he was "acting like an idiot".
The "Fun Dad" Remark: After initially agreeing to "voluntary roadside maneuvers" (sobriety tests), Raiola changed his mind and was handcuffed. After being placed in handcuffs, Raiola told the officers, "My kids are in the trunk," according to reports.
Discovery: Officers opened the trunk to find three children inside. It was noted that it was freezing temperatures outside at the time, but thankfully the children were found unharmed.
Evidence: Several bottles of alcohol were recovered from the interior of the vehicle.
Charges: Raiola was arrested on charges of:
Suspicion of DUI
Reckless driving
Three counts of child abuse
Failure to provide valid insurance 
Child Protective Services was contacted, and the children were released to family members.

comment_text: Let me begin my comments by congratulating the House of representative that has a larger membership than the Senate, for passing their version of the electoral reform laws. What I noticed in some of the senate sections is usually differences in the use of words. Electronic transfer, for example, is always used when funds are moved from one account to another, while electronic transmission has to do with the movement of data. This clearly showed that the word transfer is inappropriately used in this case. For a clear understanding and avoidance of misinterpretation, maybe the senators ought to relate to what these words represent in their own dialect during the debate to avoid this type of public embarrassment. The last time, it was changes made between the passed Tax reform laws and the gazetted version. Why can the national assembly get it right before open sessions? Matters such as electoral reform should never have partisan implications because these are laws that affect all political parties. It is not unusual for the Senate and the House of Representatives to have different versions of the law passed in both chambers. It takes a combined review by both houses before the final laws are adopted. Both houses have to get together to agree on all aspects of the laws.  Media houses' presentation is not the solution by members is not the solution. The floor of the two houses is where all members have to show their differences in opinion and the spirit of bi-partisan resolutions, not in the media. It is wrong for the minority to always try to seek public sympathy by showing up in media houses and press conferences. There are times the minority seeks full endorsement of ideas from the majority members before votes are taken. I have never witnessed any votes won by the minority from both houses. For the general public, the only grounds where bipartisan agreement exists all the time are the salary, allowances and benefits for the members of the two houses, unfortunately. That is not how parliaments around the world operate.
label: Argumentative

William Harrison Hays Sr. (November 5, 1879 – March 7, 1954) was an American politician, and member of the Republican Party. 

As chairman of the Republican National Committee from 1918 to 1921, Hays managed the successful 1920 presidential campaign of Warren G. Harding. Harding then appointed Hays to his cabinet as his first Postmaster General. 

He resigned from the cabinet in 1922 to become the first chairman of the Motion Picture Producers and Distributors of America. 

As chairman, Hays oversaw the promulgation of the Motion Picture Production Code (informally known as the Hays Code), which spelled out a set of moral guidelines for the self-censorship of content in American cinema.

comment_text:
After the fall of the Soviet Union, Cuba also had to deal with the loss of their oil supplier.   It was difficult but they survived by bringing agriculture into city lots, dividing farms into smaller farms that could be farmed with animal labour and encouraged citizens to leave for these micro farms.  They even visited pioneer historic tourist sites such as Calgary's Heritage Village where they still farm small plots of land with horses for the tourists.  The Cubans will just have to do this again till they can establish trade for oil.  

Canada still trades with Cuba but is limited with their pipelines and ports for exporting oil and Europe and China are already shifting their oil from the US to Canada so until new pipelines are built and the ports improved, Cuba can not count on Canada for the oil.  

If Russia provides the oil, it's likely that the US would embargo it.  

It may be that the US will offer it's light crude if Cuba agrees to becoming a vassal state though Cuba's refineries likely can't refine the US light crude just as the US can't refine it's own light crude. comment_text:
Listening to this discussion, what struck me wasn’t what you noticed, but the framework you kept trying to impose on the show.
This halftime performance wasn’t built as a hero’s narrative or a clean, slogan-ready protest. It was built as cultural storytelling. A mural, not a manifesto.

The “walled-off” opening wasn’t a staging quirk it was the point. Protected space first, then public space. In a country obsessed with surveilling bodies, borders, and language, that choice mattered. Before being judged, the story began with community.

When the stage opened, it didn’t reveal one man demanding the spotlight. It revealed a people: farmers, vendors, elders, boxers, jewelers, families, children. Not caricatures, contributors. Not a monolith a living culture. That’s why calling it “messy” misses the intention. It wasn’t disorder. It was communal.

The labyrinth mattered too. At first the audience could hear the music but not fully see inside. That mirrors the immigrant experience exactly , your labor feeds the nation, your culture fills the air, yet you remain unseen until the walls come down and suddenly you’re “entertainment.”

The expectation that Bad Bunny should have delivered an overt, headline friendly political statement also deserves scrutiny. America has grown comfortable demanding that Brown and Black  artists turn joy into a briefing and then absorb the backlash. Refusing that trap is itself a political choice.

The politics were there: a Spanish-language halftime show on the biggest U.S. broadcast; Puerto Rico’s power crisis embodied through choreography; a stage filled with every shade and hue of Latin identity; family, marriage, elders, children presented as a direct rebuttal to dehumanization.

When he named the countries of the Americas, he quietly corrected the idea that the United States owns the word “America.” And when he said “we’re still here,” it wasn’t spectacle it was survival.

This show didn’t offer scandal. It offered presence.

And in a moment when cruelty is rewarded and belonging is questioned, choosing to tell a story of community, dignity, and joy without turning pain into performance is not weakness. It’s restraint. And it’s powerful.

He didn’t give  scandal. He gave a portrait.

And he closed it with the oldest argument in the world: love will outlive hate because hate is a fire that burns the house down, and love is what rebuilds it.
label: Argumentative

This is exactly why it’s so important for us, as an audience, to do our homework and not let two people who have no idea what they’re talking about control the narrative. comment_text:
MAGA consistently activates nearly every marker of archetypal possession, while most Democratic messaging activates some of them intermittently, usually instrumentally, not as the movement’s core operating system.

That distinction matters.

⸻

Step 1: Everyone uses archetypes sometimes

All mass politics does this. No exceptions.

Examples across the spectrum:
	•	Moral binaries
	•	Crisis framing
	•	Villain narratives
	•	Sacred values
	•	Identity reinforcement

These are unavoidable in democratic persuasion.

So the question is not:
“Do they ever do this?”

The real question is:
Is the movement structurally dependent on these mechanisms to survive?

⸻

Step 2: What makes MAGA different structurally

MAGA is not just a political coalition.
It is a meaning system.

That’s the key distinction.

Let’s run the checklist you’re intuitively seeing.

⸻

Step 3: Archetypal possession checklist

1. Golden Age myth

Checked, constantly.
“Again” is literally the thesis statement.

The past is invoked as:
	•	Stronger
	•	Safer
	•	More moral
	•	More unified

This is not an occasional rhetorical flourish.
It is the emotional engine.

⸻

2. Strongman centrality

Checked.

The movement is leader-symbol fused.

Criticism of the leader is experienced as:
	•	Personal attack
	•	Identity attack
	•	Moral betrayal

That is archetypal inflation, not ordinary partisanship.

Democrats replace leaders frequently. MAGA does not.

⸻

3. Permanent emergency framing

Checked.

The threat is:
	•	Existential
	•	Immediate
	•	Non-negotiable
	•	Totalizing

There is no stable “normal” phase, only crisis.

That is psychologically essential for authoritarian movements.

⸻

4. Shadow externalization

Checked, repeatedly.

Blame is displaced onto:
	•	Immigrants
	•	Elites
	•	Media
	•	“Globalists”
	•	Internal traitors

Importantly, failures are never internal.
They are always sabotage.

This is classic projection.

⸻

5. Anti-ambiguity intolerance

Checked.

Traits include:
	•	Hostility to nuance
	•	Distrust of institutions
	•	Suspicion of expertise
	•	Preference for slogans
	•	Binary loyalty tests

Complexity is framed as deception.

That is a hallmark of mythic regression.

⸻

6. Loyalty over principle

Checked.

Positions shift without breaking allegiance.

What matters is:
	•	Who says it
	•	Not whether it contradicts yesterday

That tells you the movement is identity-first, not policy-first.

⸻

Step 4: How this differs from Democratic failures

Democrats absolutely:
	•	Use fear framing
	•	Oversimplify
	•	Moralize
	•	Engage in tribal rhetoric
	•	Use false dichotomies at times

But structurally:
	•	Leadership is decentralized
	•	Internal dissent exists
	•	Coalition is pluralistic
	•	No single myth binds all factions
	•	No single figure carries symbolic destiny

This produces dysfunction, hypocrisy, and messaging incoherence.

But it also prevents total archetypal possession.

Pluralism is messy.
It is also protective.

⸻

Step 5: Why MAGA “checks all the boxes”

Because it is doing psychological labor, not just political work.

It provides:
	•	Identity
	•	Belonging
	•	Moral clarity
	•	Emotional containment
	•	Status restoration
	•	Meaning in chaos

As long as it performs those functions better than alternatives, it will persist regardless of facts, failures, or contradictions.

That’s the uncomfortable truth.

⸻

Step 6: The most important implication

You cannot defeat a meaning system with criticism alone.

You have to outgrow it.

That means:
	•	Rebuilding dignity without domination
	•	Restoring belonging without exclusion
	•	Offering pride without supremacy
	•	Providing meaning without mythic enemies
	•	Allowing complexity without contempt

Until that happens, the archetype will keep reasserting itself.
label: Argumentative

comment_text: Bolaji Abdullahi, you are totally a novice as to how politics works and the expectations of the voters. During the 2023 elections, you were running your mouth in different directions. The opposition party in Nigeria, as seen from far off across the Atlantic, has been fantastic in their criticism of the ruling party, which is expected, but what they have not been able to perfect themselves in is providing solutions or alternatives to policies being implemented by the current administration. They have always shortchanged the voters, the dividend of democracy, under the pretence that they are not the ruling party. Who wants to hear that after voting for someone to be elected? If the opposition parties go into the 2027 elections based on candidates without evidence of how successfully they were in tackling non-political national crises like the insecurity, they will simply be booed. There non- non-APC elected officials from different political parties today, such as governors, national and state representatives, who promised the voters one thing or the other during the 2023 elections. They should be able to show how well they did from 2023-2027. Past failed politicians have always reappeared as if they have been born again, and they are now with new energy and ideas to change the country. The APC did the same thing. Now we have ADC with some of the pioneers of APC coming as the right Messiah in the 2027 elections. Now, who is the fool, the politicians or the voters? The best way to get around this superiority is for all elected officials to fulfill the promises made to the voters when elected. The president is not the only elected person during general elections.

comment_text: Listening to the guest, he is basically telling Nigerians that while the original laws passed are intact, the gazette alteration is enough to suspend the implementation of the laws. I think that is dumb because no court cites the gazette as the law, but simply a documented version, which can easily be corrected if any discrepancies are found. That is exactly what the president has promised the nation in his speech. For the purposes of fact-finding and for the record, it makes sense to find out how the discrepancies occurred. What is surprising is how this issue is being politicize that even leaders of the various political parties whose representatives passed the laws are now totally against the tax reform, alleging it to be a punishment for the masses. On the contrary, there are no alternative tax reform laws from the opposition.  The opposition is gearing up to make the tax reform laws an issue in the 2027 election. It is hoped they will present their own version to the public by that time. The excess of criticism lies in having a better alternative or solution. As long as the original tax laws passed are not altered, all that needs to be done is to update the gazette accordingly. Yes, it is important to know who altered the document.

comment_text:
The Guardian Newspaper: "close to 90 million Americans, roughly 36% of the eligible voting age population, did not vote"
"The 2024 US presidential election had been widely characterized as one of the most consequential political contests in recent US history. Although turnout was high for a presidential election – almost matching the levels of 2020 – it is estimated that close to 90 million Americans, roughly 36% of the eligible voting age population, did not vote. This number is greater than the number of people who voted for either Donald Trump or Kamala Harris."

76m voted Trump. 75m voted Harris. 90m registered did not vote at all. 75+90 = 165m who did not vote for Trump. So how is 76m "51% of the total electorate?"

76m is just one fifth of THE TOTAL POPULATION of 352 million people .. not including undocumented immigrants. So Trump is in power on behalf of just one fifth of the U.S. population. How can this sham be described as a "democratic system of Government?"
label: Argumentative

comment_text: I am surprised that the guest has changed his mind about the constitution. There was a time when he said in an interview that amending the constitution can be done in a very limited time. He suggested at that time that all that was needed was to go through the document step by step, take out what does work any more and replace it with what works. New areas that need to be added can also be done accordingly. I reasoned with him at the time because he is an experienced lawyer qualified enough to give an opinion of the constitution. A brand new constitution will take a long time to be agreed on. Like Seun just joked about there are a lot of Nigerians that the constitution really means nothing to when they can not feed themselves. A general election is coming up in 2027 and people are talking about a brand new constitution. It appears this conversation only surfaces when there is a new president or an election is just coming up. There is also a cry for more states. I reade in a news paper today that the national assembly constitution amendment committee has a request to increase the number of states to 55. What a joke? The United States that Nigeria copied the the state system of government only have 50 states. Currently not all the 36 states in the country can really  deliver services to the people. Nigeria is becoming a country driven by sentiments. Very soon every town in the country would want to be self governed.

comment_text:
The Guatemala syphilis experiments were United States-led human experiments conducted in Guatemala from 1946 to 1948. The experiments were led by physician John Charles Cutler, who also participated in the late stages of the Tuskegee syphilis experiment. Doctors infected 1,300 people, including at least 600 soldiers and people from various impoverished groups (including, but not limited to, sex workers, orphans, inmates of mental hospitals, and prisoners) with syphilis, gonorrhea, and chancroid, without the informed consent of the subjects. Only 700 of them received treatment. In total, 5,500 people were involved in all research experiments, of whom 83 died by the end of 1953, though it is unknown whether or not the injections were responsible for all these deaths.[1] Serology studies continued until 1953 involving the same vulnerable populations in addition to children from state-run schools, an orphanage, and rural towns. However, the intentional infection of patients ended with the original study.

On October 1, 2010, the U.S. President, Secretary of State, and Secretary of Health and Human Services[2] formally apologized to Guatemala for the ethical violations. Guatemala condemned the experiment as a crime against humanity. Multiple unsuccessful lawsuits have since been filed in the US.[3][4][5]

Susan Mokotoff Reverby of Wellesley College uncovered information about these experiments in Cutler's archived papers in 2005 while researching the Tuskegee syphilis study. She shared her findings with United States government officials.[6][7] Francis Collins, the NIH director at the time of the revelations, called the experiments "a dark chapter in history of medicine" and commented that modern rules prohibit conducting human subject research without informed consent.[8]

comment_text:
Still only about a third of the way through, but just got to your section on the when you debated Kirk at Brown which inspired me to share my experience with him.

At the end of high school I was a very different person I am today and would probably say I was moderate-right and because of this I ended up at Liberty University. (For those uninformed it's essentially THE Conservative Christian University in the United States, founded by Jerry Falwell, a key player in the rise of the religious right in the 70s and 80s) Throughout my college experience I had slowly drifted to the left primarily in seeing a blatant contradiction between the right saying they loved their neighbor in religious contexts but seeing it far from applied in their treatment of those different from them. (Particularly those that I knew were LGBT) Because of this I questioned if I really wanted to be a part of a faith that valued political power over the actual teachings of Jesus. January of 2020 Charlie Kirk and other members of TPUSA came to Liberty to speak at Convocation (which is mandatory for all on-campus students and happens every Wednesday and Friday) and they speak on various random talking points without any real structure. (I think at this point Erika and Charlie had just started dating and Falwell Jr. made a joke about having them married on the spot there.) I barely listen because I could care less at this point. Fast forward to about a month later Charlie and Erika are back at Liberty. I don't think they had actually been scheduled to speak at Convocation because they squeezed them in with another non-political speaker in the same time slot. To do this they cut out the worship section of convocation in order to put Charlie in last minute. I know it sounds ridiculous, but at the time I valued the worship section because I knew no matter how bad the (usually political) speaker was at Convocation, there was usually at least a worship set that I did take comfort in. This to me was a heavy marking point that the right cared more about politics rather than Christ and was a major part of me walking away from the faith at the time.

The short of it, Kirk unironically played a big role in me walking away from Christianity which I find both deeply sad and ironic.

Despite all this I eventually circled back to a faith in Christianity (which I'm aware is probably painfully obvious in my wording) but one that is nothing like what Liberty was trying to brainwash me to be. I came out of that propaganda machine deeply leftist.

I guess the final joke is on the right because even their universities still spit out socialists.

What I keep returning to is the funding structure; wealthy donors fund an organization that trains young people to advocate for policies that benefit wealthy donors. At first I thought "this is circular". But then I thought "all successful systems are circular". Amazon invests in Anthropic, Anthropic spends on Amazon's cloud. Donors fund Turning Point, Turning point produces voters who protect donors' interests. These are just ecosystems. Virtuous circles. My wife (who is also my editor) calls this "self-licking ice cream cones" - but she can be quite negative sometimes, which I believe comes from her training in accounting. comment_text:
This video explains the concept of politics, tracing its evolution over 2,500 years and exploring its fundamental ideas through biological, psychological, philosophical, and sociological lenses.

Here's a breakdown of the video's key points:

Politics Defined by Force and Deception (0:37-2:25): At its core, politics relies on force (power) and deception (the ability to influence through language, rhetoric, and manipulation). Ultimately, words and laws are safeguarded by brute force, establishing politics as the monopoly of violence (2:03). It functions as a pendulum between the contradictory human desires for freedom and security (2:41-2:58).

Historical Development of Politics (3:00-5:10): The video divides the history of politics into five sections:
Ancient Origins (Biological Necessity) (3:07-3:33): The rise of agriculture and cities led to increased violence, necessitating hierarchical systems like the caste system in India (3:20) and Confucianism in China (3:23). Ancient Greece and Rome explored more philosophical political orders.
Middle Ages (Psychological Politics) (3:35-4:02): As empires grew, monotheism became a psychological solution to unite diverse populations under a single, immortal God, bringing a sense of justice and order.
Renaissance (Philosophical Politics) (4:03-4:25): Rationality replaced faith. Realist thinkers like Machiavelli and Hobbes advocated for absolutist politics, prioritizing power and security, while humanist thinkers like Rousseau and Voltaire sought equality and freedom.
Enlightenment (Sociological Politics) (4:26-4:46): Humanism led to socialism (emphasizing material equality) and liberalism (emphasizing freedom), creating the modern left-right political divide.
Modern Era (Biological Politics) (4:47-5:04): Utilitarianism proposed that politics should maximize happiness, while nationalism focused on promoting community and belonging, forming a second left-right divide.

Humanity as Political Animals (5:29-10:20): Humans are inherently political animals due to their linguistic abilities and innate desire for community (5:34-6:20). The conflicting desires for scarce resources lead to conflict, which evolution has addressed through physical violence and, uniquely for humans, the creation of tools like politics to manage and direct violence (9:49-10:20).

Key Political Thinkers and Ideas: The video delves into the ideas of numerous influential political thinkers, including:
Aristotle (5:32): Coined "political animals."
Chanakya (20:26): Emphasized rational, pragmatic leadership, intelligence, and the material prosperity of the kingdom for ancient Indian politics.
Plato (32:02) and Cicero (33:18): Advocated for justice, honesty, and honor in the Western tradition.
Machiavelli (1:05:11): Argued for ruthlessness and power maintenance, believing "the end justifies the means" (1:05:22).
Hobbes (1:10:51): Believed in a strong sovereign to control inherently selfish humans and maintain order.
John Locke (1:17:51): Championed individual freedom and the protection of private property as the government's primary role.
Rousseau (1:21:55): Blamed private property for societal ills and advocated for equality, influencing the French Revolution.
David Hume (1:23:08): Emphasized practical utility and happiness as the state's role.
Karl Marx (1:31:34): Argued for the removal of capitalism and the establishment of communism to achieve material equality.
Alexis de Tocqueville (1:48:10): Saw socialism as a threat to liberty due to its focus on materialism, opposition to private property, and emphasis on the collective over the individual.
Johann Gottfried Herder (2:09:59): Considered a pioneer of nationalism, promoting community and belonging.
Michel Foucault (2:15:00): Explored the relational nature of power and how modern institutions control individuals.

The video concludes by reiterating that human politics continues to grapple with the biological urges for utility (happiness) and nationalism (belonging) (2:26:40-2:26:45), suggesting a potential future where humanity forms a single, unified colony.
label: Informational

comment_text:
We solved our binfluencer  problem in the easiest way possible.

I made a simple website, you enter your email address, and suddenly at 5PM on the day before you'll get an email reminding you to put out the corresponding bin.

Costs me about £2 a month to run, and I've had people from a few streets over that have a different bin schedule if I can make them a website too.
I've so far passed on as it means me downloading the PDF for the area, putting all the bin collection dates in a list with the relevant bins manually(the format doesn't work well for scraping, I've tried, you get about 80% accuracy which doesn't save any time given the need to check it and fix the mistakes), and I need to do this 3 times a year(January for the new year, around July because the last half of the year sometimes changes, and then again in November when they finally decide the December schedule).
It's a bunch of effort for something I really think the council should fix. It's not my responsibility to do their job for the entire town.
label: Argumentative

I can understand lying by omission which might be the bridge strategy he's talking about. You wouldn't want to release sensitive information so carelessly. What would constitute sensitive is whether or not the public is firm enough to handle it. If it causes unnecessary trouble then it's better to just leave it out and build a "bridge" for the sake of simplicity. This I do understand and that's actually really important. They did it a little too much that it may have been too embarrassing to just admit they were on some wavelength that just made no sense and it was just too late to turn back out of sheer embarrassment. That's all I can really make out of it. If they regress or admit that they're actually lost then they risk being embarrassed so they just keep plowing ahead. They at least are running in some form of logic it's just that they're already too deep they see it better to make the ends of their original goals. So even though they're wrong they just never recovered. You can get away with lying if you recover from it. But they never did. When I say "they" I'm referring to American politicians. They simply kept creating more and more "bridges" without any real plan. And then lo and behold Mr. Trump who literally talks to the public like he's got a 6th grade reading level, which he doesn't I'm not sure why he dumbs himself down maybe to make himself so clare the media won't be able to twist his words, it just kind of makes me laugh sometimes because he's smarter when he's actually speaking with full intent. 

The first time Trump was gaining popularity I thought it was a joke. I wasn't offended by him but I thought it ridiculous for him to be president I assumed it was out of his depth. After his first term he didn't exactly earn my full respect. I would give him a C+ for his first term which honestly is still pretty good. C+ feels almost like a let down and I can definitely see why he was so upset when he lost against Biden because in his head he would've made the C+ into so much more and I believe he had earnest intentions. It was a good thing that he lost to further clarify the difference what the trade offs actually were. Currently I don't think he could've done any better for his first year and give him B-.

comment_text:
Federalists presents a specific, concrete document the Constitution as the solution to the nation's problems.
Anti-Federalists mostly offered critiques of the proposed system without providing a unified, viable alternative. They were essentially fighting for the status quo (the weak Articles of Confederation) which most recognized was failing, or arguing for amendments that weren't formalized into a counter-proposal.. The group was diverse and rarely united on a single vision. Some wanted minor changes to the Articles, others wanted a total "redo/reform", and they lacked a singular leader or platform to rival the Federalist organization. 
Because they focused heavily on tearing down the Constitution 
fearing it would lead to a "consolidated" government, a standing army, and monarchical power they were viewed as OBSTRUCTIONIST🙌👣🔍⚖️..
Their platform was rooted in fear of central power rather than a structured alternative, making it harder to convince the public..

comment_text:
Norms Trump Disrupted or Broke 

1. Respect for Election Results
	•	Historically, losing candidates conceded to preserve trust in democracy (e.g., Al Gore in 2000).
	•	Trump refused to concede in 2020, spread false claims of widespread fraud, and pressured officials to overturn results.

2. Separation of Personal Business and Presidency
	•	Past presidents put assets in blind trusts to avoid conflicts of interest.
	•	Trump kept ownership of the Trump Organization, had foreign and domestic business dealings, and profited from his hotels during his presidency.

3. Use of Pardons
	•	Traditionally used to correct injustices or show mercy.
	•	Trump often pardoned close allies (e.g., Roger Stone, Paul Manafort, Michael Flynn), making it seem more self-protective than impartial.

4. Truthfulness in Public Communication
	•	Presidents sometimes spin facts, but Trump’s sheer volume of false or misleading claims (tens of thousands documented) was unprecedented.

5. Respect for Independent Institutions
	•	Normally, presidents avoid pressuring agencies like the DOJ, FBI, or intelligence services.
	•	Trump publicly pressured them to serve his personal or political interests and fired officials who resisted.

6. Peaceful Transition of Power
	•	One of the strongest U.S. traditions is a smooth handoff to the next president.
	•	Trump boycotted Biden’s inauguration and his rhetoric fueled the Jan. 6th Capitol attack, disrupting this norm.

7. Presidential Decorum
	•	Past presidents avoided open insults and vulgar attacks.
	•	Trump regularly attacked political opponents, the press, judges, and even members of his own administration with harsh personal insults on Twitter.

8. Avoiding Foreign Influence in Domestic Politics
	•	Traditionally, presidents keep foreign governments out of U.S. campaigns.
	•	Trump encouraged foreign interference (e.g., asking Ukraine for dirt on Biden, publicly asking Russia for Clinton’s emails).

9. Transparency with Finances & Tax Returns
	•	Since Nixon, presidents released tax returns for accountability.
	•	Trump refused, fought legal battles to keep them hidden, and was later shown to pay very little in taxes some years.

10. Commitment to Allies & Global Leadership
	•	Previous presidents upheld long-standing alliances (NATO, G7, etc.).
	•	Trump weakened or threatened to leave alliances, praised authoritarian leaders, and treated allies with hostility.

comment_text:
How much land has Russia Japan and China bought within the United States according to information provided by Google :
Foreign entities own a total of approximately 45.8 million acres of U.S. agricultural land, which accounts for about 3.6% of all privately held agricultural land. The amount of land owned by Russia, Japan, and China is a very small fraction of this total, particularly for Russia. 
Here are the approximate land holdings for each country:
	•	Russia: Russian investors and entities own roughly 100 acres of land in total across the United States. Land owned by Russian, Chinese, and Iranian investors combined makes up only 0.03% of American farmland, and Russia's share is minimal. The Russian government itself does not own land outside of official embassies.
	•	Japan: Japanese entities hold an interest in approximately 283,000 acres of U.S. agricultural land (as of 2023 data). This includes properties like orchards and coffee-growing acreage. In terms of total foreign-owned land, Japan typically ranks around 13th or lower.
	•	China: Chinese investors and companies own slightly more than Japan, with figures ranging from approximately 277,000 to 384,000 acres, depending on the reporting year and data corrections. This amount is less than 1% of all foreign-held land in the U.S. Most of this land is tied to specific companies like Smithfield Foods (a pork processor acquired by a Chinese firm in 2013) or past wind farm projects in Texas that were later halted by state law. 
For an official breakdown of foreign land ownership by state and country, you can use the USDA Foreign Owned Agricultural Lands interactive map (https://www.google.com/url?sa=i&source=web&rct=j&url=https://www.arcgis.com/apps/dashboards/9c10b047a2794edc8e9671d0d758592c&ved=2ahUKEwjolbOO2bmRAxW-kmoFHQrcH6kQy_kOegQIBhAB&opi=89978449&cd&psig=AOvVaw18rGlONcQYybzaE2bQEXs2&ust=1765685476993000).

comment_text:
Politics 101
 
Tell people what they want to hear. This is why sucking up works. Has to be done with more skill with intelligent people, less so with the ignorant. The ignorant want their ass kissed.
Feed their emotions back to them. You are on their side. Bosses want to have some PhD scientist tell them they are right. People who do this are paid very well. Their feelings of persecution are useful. They are victims and you are here to tell them that there “is a conspiracy” just like they think.
You will be attacked if you are independent and competent. Use the 90% rule: be 90% as competent as your boss. Mess up some things so your boss can attack you and feel superior. Don’t mess up anything that really matters.
There is always a scapegoat. Make sure it isn’t you.

comment_text:
2023 January 09 Part 1 Comment on Youtube HBR – The Best Way to Play Office Politics.

This interview with the authors of the book: Being the Boss (Professors Linda Hill and Kent Lineback) by HBR through Ms. Sarah Green is like a very thick, moist and delicious black forest cholate cake. There is so much to chew on and learn from.  As an expedient means, politics, even its sub-discipline, office politics, is such a deep and controversial expedient means to explore. I am unsure whether it is possible to condense it in a 16-minute dialogue. Yet, in spite of this constraint, the dialogue between Ms. Green, and Professors Hill and Lineback, delivered. Just as a moist, thick and delicious chocolate black forest cake does.  

From the authors, as expounded by Prof. Linda Hill, the dynamics of office politics are: 1. Diversity of Perspectives 2. Interdependence of players 3. Scarcity of Resources. Further, one needs to create 3 political networks to succeed. They are: 1. Operational network (your team) 2. Strategic network (SWOT team) 3. Developmental network (mentors).

Prof. Kent Lineback also tugged an emotional string with these humanistic statements: “… powerlessness corrupts.” “If you don’t have power, then you can’t stand up for what you believe is right”. “You need to understand how you make other people feel, when they’re with you.” And last –“… it didn’t work that well”, when he didn’t give office politics the significance it deserves in pursuit of an objective.

As a suggestion, all leadership books should be supplemented by a 15-minute interview, unscripted, like this one. It is where the rubber (authors) meets the road (interviewer). And if you factor in the following 1) attention span of “readers” seem shorter now and 2) reading time is even less available vis-à-vis other attention getters, then the interview format is really the “primary“ reading time. The “secondary” reading time is… how does one explain this better… a second…. , the interview is akin to eating a very heavy slice of the cake for lunch. It doesn’t make you really feel full going back to the office but it’s thick enough to get through the afternoon. However, because it is so savory, it whets your appetite for more come post dinner. It awakens a compelling need to consume the rest of it, at a more appropriate time when you are free of other chores and distractions. As a book marketing teaser, this interview is very persuasive.

Further. for those who will never have access to this book, by force of circumstances or otherwise, this interview is a “book” by itself.  Probably, in the future, hardbacks and audiobooks will be receiving stiff competition from “interview books”. The interview is the “Main Book” and the paperback or hardbound, or audiobook is for “further reading” and available by subscription plan. Respectfully, what do you think? 

Great job to the HBR team, including those marvelous people operating anonymously behind-the-scenes. Great job. Warmest congratulations.

(Comment Part1- reposted at my linkedin page: www.linkedin.com/in/emmanuelmatuco)  Part 2 will be my thoughts about Office Politics.  Emmanuel Matuco

comment_text:
Kissing ass to get ahead is just stupid.  I use hard work, frugal living, and compound interest (dividend stocks, high yield ETFs, etc) to get ahead.  I'd rather own 20,000 shares of Coca-Cola and live in a double wide than to live in a mansion and be cash-poor.  When I buy a car, I drive it until it dies.  I chose to never marry or have kids because I know women care about social status and I'm not into that.  I've always made it a point to be completely classless.  I could have $100 million dollars and I'd own tons of blue chip dividend stocks, laundromats, car washes, etc but I'd live in a very modest house and drive the same Corolla I have now.  Money can't change me because I just enjoy being myself.  I feel zero sense of loss in living this way because if I want something and the trifecta I mentioned a moment ago isn't enough to procure my desired outcome, then I just take that as a sign from the universe that it wasn't meant to be.  

Having said all that, I'd like to leave you guys with a few thoughts:

1)  Never, ever do anything that makes you not like yourself.  If kissing ass to get a raise or promotion would cause you to not like yourself, don't do it regardless of how much money is involved.  

2)  When contemplating a given action, if that action doesn't live in your heart and soul, don't do it.  

3)  Send out positive energy into the universe.  Donate to your favorite charity.  Nothing is ever lost in the universe; and if you send out positive energy, it will eventually return to you.  

3)  There are tons of ways to get ahead without kissing ass.  Use compound interest.  Start a website.  Sell on eBay.  Think of an idea and get it patented.  

4)  I did myself a great favor when I was 16 years old.  I decided that I wasn't going to play the status game.  I wasn't going to go for the best house and the fanciest car; I was simply going to live a modest frugal lifestyle and invest my surplus at compound interest, and occassionally give to charity.  When I made this decision, I exempted myself from a lifetime of stress because so much stress in life comes from trying to keep up with the Joneses.  I slammed the door shut on all of that at 16.  For those of you who haven't yet stepped outside the matrix and streamlined your priorities like I have, I suggest giving it a shot.  You can always go back to doing it your way if you don't like my way, so you got nothing to lose!

comment_text:
Can we stop saying "capitalism sucks" or "communism sucks" - because while in part you're right - we can learn from both ideas and systems that we can apply to the current system? 

Capitalism spurs innovation and enterpreneurship. It gives incentives for people to better themselves. But it gives a major advantage to wealthy families and threatens to leave the poor and lower income families in the dust. Even if they have the brains and ambition to rise up from their circumstances - any misstep, any accident, any sort of inconvenience could put them 10 steps back when they've moved 3 steps forward. Moreover, the ways it pushes people to grow is simply according to the needs of the capitalist system - barely any room to pursue their interests and hobbies unless those fit in with the current economic demand. 

Communism (theoretically) calls for the equal redistribution of wealth and the communal sharing of material wealth/abolition of private ownership. It desires to give people the means to live an egalitarian, leisurely life not constrained by economic worries, and relationships not tainted by economic gain. However in reality, true communism - the one where the state no longer is required and where everything is equal - requires a strong state to redistribute the wealth. It requires higher taxes on the rich, more public services and payments especially to the poor, state control of industries, and labour protection. (What we may call socialism). This has however - at least in unstable, developing countries (e.g. czarist Russia, Venezuela) with little to no strong democratic foundation to work on - led to horrible dictatorships who end up being corrupted with power and the wealth they seize, and leave society with more inequality to begin with. It also gives no incentive to individuals to innovate on current economic systems since the state controls all legal means of production, preventing technological progress we've made today that has made life easier for most in the world. 

Both have pros and cons so lets figure this out instead of just bashing ourselves into ideological brick walls.
label: Argumentative

Hey Heather I live in MN. It has been harrowing, outrageous,
and heartbreaking. I loved my state before but I love it even more now. Yes we have been motivated by wanting to take care of our neighbors regardless of their ethnicity or their status. Our frustration at not being protected by the law, or our police, or our legislators pretty much put the writing on the wall - we were going to have to take care of ourselves and one another. It’s more than the whistles, the car honking, and the protests. Citizens are monitoring their neighborhoods and definitely the schools to protect the children. We are delivering groceries and meals and whatever items are needed to families who don’t feel safe leaving their homes. We are giving people rides to wherever they need to go so that there is a “white” person in the car with them. So there is an enormous amount of protection and love that goes on quietly in the background at the same time as all of the things that are seen on television, but it all starts with the word neighbor. These are our neighbors and no one is gonna come and abuse them, hassle them, or take their freedom away. I know there is the saying out there of “Minnesota nice.” That saying is true. AHH how the world has failed socialism. That's not what socialism is, and even if it was if you're an average citizen you would actually get money, while only the top 1% would lose money

Socialism is about using tax dollars on the betterment of society and giving people equity in the opportunities to lead a happy and wealthy life. Capitalism is about giving people equality in the opportunities to live a wealthy and happy life 

Capitalism says "oh you're disabled? Too bad we are all equal, so you don't get special privileges" 

Socialism says "oh you're disabled? Well we have resources and laws in place where you can achieve the same amount of wealth as a non disabled with the same amount of effort" 

Socialism isn't crippling others for the sake of equality, it's boosting others for the sake of equality. But since everything costs money, the money needs to come from somewhere, therefore the rich can take a few less yachts and cruises to help a child with lukemia with their tax dollars

In every system someone suffers, it's just that in capitalism the poor suffer, while in socialism the rich suffer. comment_text: This Grenell statement isn’t about “protecting a nonprofit” or “saving a national treasure”—it’s a loyalty script stapled to a cultural institution. It praises a political figure as if that’s the venue’s job, then flips the story by calling an artist’s voluntary withdrawal “intolerance,” like basic freedom of association is some kind of moral failure. The “costly to a nonprofit” line is guilt-as-leverage, and the blame-shift to “elements on the left” is classic scapegoating meant to erase the artist’s agency and paint dissent as outside manipulation. And the kicker is the hypocrisy: accusing others of intimidation while floating legal/financial punishment for opting out is the intimidation. Dress it up in “national treasure” language all you want—this is pressure, branding, and narrative control, not stewardship.

comment_text:
Most People Have No Idea How Close We Came

Most people have absolutely no idea what was happening beneath the surface when President Trump was handed this country. They only remember headlines. They do not understand the trajectory we were already on.

When President Trump took office, the United States was in a spiral. It was a controlled demolition.

The U.S. dollar was actively losing its grip as the world’s reserve currency. The rise of BRICS was accelerating, with nations openly planning alternatives to dollar dominance. Manufacturing had been gutted and hollowed out for decades. Entire regions of America had been sacrificed on the altar of globalization. We were bleeding jobs, manufacturing, leverage, respect, and sovereignty. Our borders were being invaded daily…with no end in sight.

At the same time, the country was being quietly aligned with United Nations Agenda 2030 and the World Economic Forum vision of managed decline. Centralized control. Global governance. Reduced national independence. This was not a theory. It was policy direction. We were being walked into a controlled collapse by design.

President Trump did not inherit a stable system. He inherited a rigged one that was already breaking.

That is why the moves he made looked drastic to people who did not understand the moment. The tariffs were not reckless. They were necessary! They are bringing massive amounts of money back into the United States and forced long overdue trade realignments. Deals were renegotiated with China. Pressure was applied to the European Union. The era of America being the global piggy bank was challenged for the first time in generations. If these things did not happen, we would have been in a critical position and would have been like Venezuela ourselves!

Large scale investments are flowing back into the United States right now…not just from corporations, but from foreign governments that suddenly understand America is no longer asleep. Manufacturing is beginning to return. Supply chains are being rebuilt. The hemorrhaging is stopping.

This is not about optics. It is about stopping the bleeding.

Then came the internal reckoning. Wasteful spending that had become normalized for decades was finally confronted. The use of DOGE and the partnership with Elon Musk to expose inefficiencies was not a sideshow. It was a pressure release valve. Entire systems that existed only to funnel money into black holes are being challenged and exposed. And it is only the beginning!

USAID, in many cases, functioned as a laundering operation disguised as humanitarian aid. That reality is uncomfortable for people who prefer slogans over truth. But it is true. Billions were flowing with little accountability, often against America’s own interests, to NGOs and hostile organizations.

And now we are seeing the next necessary step. Securing our hemisphere. Reasserting boundaries. Reintroducing the Monroe Doctrine in a modern context. Not imperialism. Protection. Stability. Survival.

The critics do not understand this because they never understood the depth of the problem.

This country was not headed toward a mild recession. We were headed toward becoming Venezuela. Currency erosion.

Dependency. Global control. Internal collapse masked by endless spending. If President Trump had not intervened, that trajectory would have continued uninterrupted.

Uninformed people truly have no idea how close we came to economic destruction.

This was not politics. This was triage.

And whether people like his style or not, whether they agree with every decision or not, the truth remains. We were pulled back from the edge. The collapse was interrupted. The direction was reversed.
Everyone should be thanking God right now.

Because history will show that this was not about one man. It was about a moment where the trajectory of an entire nation changed.
And, we are not out of the woods yet. We need to continue to pray. This latest move was a gamble, but it was necessary. And there is a lot more that needs to be done to make this all turn around.

We need to pray that the Ukraine war comes to an end. Let’s also pray for what’s happening in Iran right now.

Let’s keep praying and believing that God is going to move in a mighty way. It’s time for us to wake up. It’s time for us to thank God. It’s time for us to pray for this great nation.🙏❤️
